title: Predicting usual residence address from admin data in the 2023 Census | Stats NZ
content:
## Predicting usual residence address from admin data in the 2023 Census
29 May 2024, 10:46am
Predicting usual residence address from admin data in the 2023 Census describes the implementation of a model to predict usual residence address from administrative (admin) data sources available within the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI).
The addresses identified using this method are part of the 2023 Census file (the census dataset), both as admin enumerations (records that are based on admin data and do not correspond to a census form response) and when we fill in missing usual residence information from census forms.
This paper first outlines the data used, then details the method, and finally presents quality measures for the method, such as consistency of the predicted admin address with 2023 Census responses. A summary of the current uses of this approach beyond the 2023 Census is also included.
- Predicting usual residence address from admin data in the 2023 Census Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1.1 MB

### Predicting usual residence address from admin data in the 2023 Census
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1.1 MB
The 2023 Census was designed as a combined census model, with alternative data sources used to supplement census form responses. Admin data is used to add records (referred to as admin enumerations) to the census file when an individual has not responded and when we are confident in the quality of the admin record.
Much of the key value of census data comes through its ability to produce statistics for detailed geographic areas. Therefore, when adding admin enumerations to the census file, we need to have confidence in where an individual usually lives, so we can place them in the correct location. High quality admin addresses lead to more people being included in the correct household or geographic area in census outputs. This, in turn, also improves the household and family census output datasets.
We have developed an approach that uses a machine-learning model to predict usual residence address for each person in the admin resident population, using the range of address sources available within the IDI. The model first produces a probability that each of the addresses listed for an individual is correct, as measured against 2023 Census responses. We then select the highest probability address for each person to align with the usual residence concept. A final adjustment is made to align particular individuals’ address with their parents’ address.
Overall, the final output of this model shows approximately 90 percent consistency with 2023 Census responses, a considerable improvement over previous methods. Other quality metrics also show similar improvements. While there may be some reduction in interpretability of how individual addresses were selected, this is outweighed by the increase in addresses being selected correctly.
In keeping with the combined census model for the 2023 Census, the addresses selected through this approach are used to supplement the usual residence variable of census responses. Furthermore, these addresses, as well as their associated address scores, are a direct input into the processes for adding admin records to the 2023 Census dataset.
Similar models have also been used to predict two other attributes for the 2023 Census – usual residence one year ago and usual residence five years ago – and for the household component of the experimental administrative population census. (See Experimental administrative population census .)
